title: Up from Slavery by Booker T Washington  Book Sysopsis

transcript.text: Yo, what's up dogs? I'm going to do a video about, um, video, um, book review of Booker T. Washington up from slavery. Um, so, um, so let me talk about Booker T. Washington first.

Booker T. Washington was the foremost African American in the, uh, turn of the century, turn of the 20th century, 1900. He was the last generation of African Americans born into slavery. He was nine years old when the, uh, when, uh, he was freed in 1865. He was born in 1856.

Um, so, the book Up from Slavery is his autobiography, telling the story of, and you see, he died as, like, a very, um, uh, you know, influential African American who's, who's, like, wheeling and dealing with all of the richest white people.

So, he, he really came up from slavery. And, uh, yeah, just tells his story. Um, so, uh, let me just do a little, really quick, uh, introduction just to who he was, and then I can, I guess, run through the chapters. Um, it's a really good book. Um, I think, I, I read the, uh, Wikipedia article about him.
And his book, um, his book, um, Up from Slavery is number three on, like, 100 most influential nonfiction books on some website that, that categorize that stuff. They're read on, uh, Wikipedia.
Um, he was, uh, I guess what you would call an apologist for the white man. He, uh, um, he, he's kind of like, uh, it was, I guess he, uh, and will, like, W.E.B.
Dubois would have been the, like, the, uh, kind of the more aggressive. So, like, um, from that generation. So, you know, Booker T. Washington would, it was like, uh, Martin Luther King Jr.
And W.B. Dubois was like Malcolm X. Um, W.B. Dubois was the one who started NAACP, the National Association of, of color people back in, like, 1910.
And, uh, Booker T. Washington was the foremost educator. He brought, uh, he, he, he started the education for black people after the emancipation.

Um, and he was, he was friends with, uh, like, all the whites. He hung out with, uh, Queen, Queen Victoria. He was close friends with the richest man in the world, the Duke of, like, Wales or something like that.

And, um, and, um, he, he, uh, he met with, like, President McKinley, President Roosevelt, and another president, Taft, I think it was, and, um, and, uh, yeah, got a lot of, you know, money from them for his schools.
So, he, uh, so here's his autobiography. So let's, let's, let's run through it and just talk about what he was, uh, writing about and, um, you know, my impressions of it.
So it's, it's, it's a really long book. It's 17 hours to power through, um, from the, from bookshouldbefree.com with them reading it to you.
Uh, 18 chapters. Uh, it's ghostwritten. It was written very, very, very well.
I was very, like, impressed that he could be such a good writer, but then I found out it was ghostwritten.
But he did, uh, six of his books were ghostwritten, and, uh, but he read, he wrote 14 books.
So I guess eight books he wrote on his own.
Um, so, chapter one, A Slave Among Slaves.
So he was, he's basically talking about, uh, what it was like being a slave.
He was born a slave, um, in, uh, in Virginia.

His mother told him that his father was a white man, so he's half black, uh, just like, um, just like that other, uh, very famous, uh, black man who wrote a book about, um, who, who actually freed, escaped from slavery.
He was, like, a generation before Booker T. Washington, um, um, oh, God, I can't remember.
Yeah, so anyway, that guy was half white, too.
I guess the white man liked to have sex with the black women.
Um, but he never knew, that's all he knew.
He just knew his, his daddy was some plantation owner from, like, another plantation.
So he probably came and partied with her owner and had sex with her, and then she popped out a baby.
Um, so, yeah, when he was a child, he had an older brother who was, like, six years older than him or something.
And, uh, and he lived, he was born in a, uh, in just, like, a one-room, um, cabin with no windows.
And his mother was the cook for all the plantation slaves.
And so she worked out of that cabin, and they also lived in the cabin.
Uh, he was one of the plantation workers.
He had to wear a flax, a flax shirt.
That was the slave outfit.
Um, I forgot to, like, Wikipedia or Google what flax shirt is.
I think it's, like, just really basic.
The cheapest, like, shirts that they would make those days.
Um, and, but he said it was excruciatingly painful to wear.
He had to, uh, like, he was, like, putting on, um, like, like, a shirt of needles.
Um, but his brother was really nice and was older than him and offered to wear it for a week to break it in for him before he put it on because he would have rather just gone, gone shirtless.
Um, he said that slaves were pretty, he said his owners were nice, but, um, but that basically, you know, slaves are basically like animals.
Like, if you bought a new slave, it was basically like buying a new, a new horse or something.
People would, would, would, you know, just be like, oh, here's our new slave.
Okay, get to work there, boy.
And that's it, you know.
They wouldn't, you know, wouldn't try to, you know, treat him like humans or anything beyond that.
But, uh, since he was born into slavery, see, and I've, I've, this is another thing that I've, uh, heard, that I read from, uh, the other slave books.
Um, um, oh, I can't remember what they're called, but, but yeah, um, you know, slaves weren't told anything.
They were just told to get to work.
So they, you know, as far as they knew, they were just, slavery was something that always existed and, you know, they didn't know that there was anything other than that.

And, um, but he, but he did, um, wish that he was white and, um, and what he really wished about, and he envied the white people, but what he really wished, envied, uh, was to be able to read and write.

He was, he said his dream was to be able to read and write, but he thought the chances of being able to, like, and, and being able to read and write would have been like equivalent to him as like getting into paradise.
So kind of like, you know, something you could only dream of and would probably never happen, but it would be wonderful to happen.
Um, he tried, he tried to get other people to teach him.
Um, I don't know if this was before or after they became emancipated.
I guess it was after, um, but he was kind of shy and, but he eventually learned to read and write.
When he was nine, uh, that's when the, uh, South lost.
And, um, they were on their plantation one day, you know, at the end of the war.
And some, you know, union soldier came up to their plantation and, and read this, um, really long dossier saying, oh, uh, you are all free.
You can all go wherever you want.
And so his mother explained to them, oh, this is our day.
We are free to go to wind.
Yay.
And she was, tears were streaming down her face.
And, and as the days leading up to the guy, given the emancipation proclamation to them,
The slaves were singing more at night and were really happy.
And so after they became free, um, some slaves left, um, especially the slaves who didn't like their owners.
But the slaves who did like their owners, you know, they didn't really know anything about the world.
They didn't know how to manage, you know, navigate through the world.
So they just kind of came up with an agreement with their boss, with their owners to, uh, set up some kind of system where they would pay on something.
And, uh, they would just stay on there, you know, so not that much really changed.
Um, so after he was set free, she got married to another black man and they moved to West Virginia.
And, um, but his stepfather, and he wanted to go to school, but his stepfather didn't let him cause he wanted to him to help, help, you know, work for wages for the family.
So he got a job as a, uh, as a coal miner where he had to, he had to, after he went underground, he'd have to walk an extra quarter mile just to get to where he'd do the digging.
Um, and he's, you know, working since he was like nine.
Um, so I think that's the second chapter, chapter two, boyhood days.
Um, so, um, and that's about when they went freedom.
He, he said he named himself.
Um, when, when, when, uh, slaves went free, they usually would take the last names of their owners.
And, um, I, I think maybe he took the last name of like George Washington.

He had heard a story from George, about George Washington that he respected that like once George Washington was walking down the, um, the road with all his guys and, and, uh, a black man tipped his hat to him and he tipped his hat back to the black man.
And all the guys around him were like, Hey, what you doing tipping your hat to a black man?
And George Washington said, I'm not going to let a Negro be more of a gentleman than me.
So it kind of gives you a, uh, an idea of how white people treated black people back in those days.
You know, like if, like you wouldn't even tip your hat to them, you know, like you're an animal.
I mean, like even in these days I would, I would nod and smile at a dog, but they didn't even do that.
I think they felt threatened by him back in the days of slavery.
They had, um, they, there was a group of, uh, like, I guess a job.
I don't know if they were paid to do this or they did it on, uh, voluntarily, but usually white young men, they would go out there.
They were called the enforcers and they would go out there and just make sure that slaves weren't running away and that they were in their right plantation that they were supposed to be.
So they'd be like patrolling the streets and stuff.
And after they went free, the, um, they teamed up with a new group of people, the Ku Klux Klan that Booker T. Washington talked a lot about it, but the Ku Klux Klan like took it more seriously.
They were, they didn't want the black people to kind of come up at all.
So they would burn down their buildings and stuff.
Um, so yeah, it's kind of kind of freaky how people would just not, not talk to blacks.
Um, my kind of conceptualization of why they would, it were like that is maybe because I, you know, I think they were just threatened by black people come from Africa where it's primitive.
So they just didn't want black people to like bring that primitivity to America and destroy America or they were.
And, but it was mainly what he said.
It was mainly the, um, the, uh, the, the, like the poor white Southerners who are the ones who had that idea.
He said a lot of like Northern whites and other, uh, and also whites in the South were, he, he loved them.
They were, they wanted to help the blacks just as much as the blacks want to help themselves.
And they would even fight to try to defend the blacks when they would, um, attack them, you know, when the Ku Klux Klan would attack them.
And one of his, I think one of his like employers, a white man would like gotten a fight once and got injured by these guys.
Cause they would, they, they would hurt and even kill white people who would try to defend the black people.
And yeah, one of his employers, uh, got like permanently injured in one of these altercations.
Um, so, you know, some people like, I heard somebody say the other day, oh yeah, every, all the whites were racist back then.
Well, you know, Booker T. Washington, some of his favorite people were white.
He, there is especially one man called General Armstrong.
I can't remember his first name, but he was a general in the, uh, in the Union Army during the Civil War.
And, uh, then he started an institute, Hampton University.
And he was basically took, he met Booker T. Washington because maybe this is going to be chapter three.
Um, chapter three, a struggle for education.
Yeah.
He wanted to, um, he wanted to go and learn, learn to read when he, when he became free.
And, um, and was where he would like work and then go to, he couldn't go to day school because his stepfather wouldn't allow him.
But he envied the other black kids who did because they started to open up schools.
So he started going to night school and I think he had to walk like miles to go there.
So he'd like work in the, in the, in the coal mine during the day and then, um, and then, and then study at night.
And, uh, and then he, he, he enrolled in this new, you know, school for blacks called Hampton.
And he met, um, General Armstrong and General Armstrong was impressed with him and kind of took him under his wing.
And, uh, he said General Armstrong was like the most, the, his favorite guy in the world.
And, um, you know, and would like, he, he said he, when he started his, his, see, later on Booker Washington started his own black university, Tuskegee University.
And he said Armstrong like gave like his entire life savings to him to, uh, to get started with the, um, the university.
So he's, Booker Washington says that the epoch of blacks, when they became free and were taught how to read, it was one of the most exciting epochs like ever in the history of mankind.
Um, they, because the only people who know how to read were white.
And so there would have these, all these white volunteers who would start the schools and teach the black men how to read and write.
And, and then they could go and teach other people how to read and write.

And, uh, he had, he said that the churches would took, did a very amazing, amazing job of, uh, starting all of these schools and, um, you know, and donating their money and their time and resources to help all of the black schools get started.
Um, so, uh, so, uh, let's see, chapter four, helping others.
So then, yeah, so then he, he, after he learned, he, um, how to read, he, uh, he started to teach other people how to read.
Um, and, uh, I think he moved to Richmond.
I don't know where Hampton is, but, um, he, he was originally homeless when he went to Richmond.
I mean, he wanted to get room and board and, uh, but none of the people would, uh, would let him have room and board if he didn't have the money up front.
So he had to be homeless for a while.
And he thought that was unfair because I guess that's what they, they, they gave a better deal to the white people.
They would let him move in and then could pay him later, but not with him.
And so he, uh, but then he got a job as a waiter, but he didn't, he didn't do a very good job.
So he, um, he had to learn and then he kind of worked himself up the ranks.
And, um, and, uh, eventually let's see here.
What's chapter five reconstructing period.
Um, yeah.
So he talks about reconstruction.
I want to, I should listen to these chapters again.
Chapter five and six are really interesting.
Um, yeah.
So basically Booker T Washington's, um, idea about blacks was that the most important thing that blacks needed was an education.
Uh, and he stressed that liberal arts education, which is like, um, the education, you know, like languages and history and, um, just to stuff to make them like well-rounded citizens.
Who knew, you know, like ancient Greek and Latin and this kind of stuff so that they could be, um, you know, cultured, know what's going on in the world.

And he, he thought, and of course, also teaching trades like, uh, teaching and, and farming and stuff so that they could, uh, they could be more productive members of society and especially, you know, make money, be productive members and make their own money and, uh, have their own families and houses and all that kind of stuff.
And that's what he was focused on.
That's what he thought that they needed to, um, to only go after.

And he, so he, he was in support of, uh, Jim Crow laws, which is, I think Jim Crow was probably a congressman who supported all this stuff, which is segregation, which was from the fall of slavery until, um, until, um, the fall of segregation in the sixties.

So for like, for like a hundred years, they were segregated, uh, but he was supported that because he thought that if they tried to get, uh, desegregated, it was just too much for the whites to handle and, um, they would lose support.
So he kind of thought that they'd take baby steps.
However, um, after he died, they found out that he had been donating a lot of money without anybody knowing about it to causes for desegregation.
So if anybody had a part, because he turned a lot of black people against him, I think I'm getting a little ahead right now.
So let's just go through really quickly.
Um, chapter six is the black race and the red race.
Um, yeah, Hampton, he was, he was, uh, he went, so after he studied at Hampton, he went back there.
Armstrong called him back there to help, to help teach, uh, a native Americans.
And he was, he said, I remember him mentioning about native Americans that native Americans thought that they were better than the whites.
And I've heard that from other stories to books too, that the native Americans really thought that, you know, their, their, their lifestyle was superior.
They were more, um, more free, you know, more, just led more interesting lives, but they thought that, that they thought they were way better than the blacks.
They didn't have any respect for blacks because of how, because the blacks allowed themselves to get enslaved.
The native Americans never allowed themselves to get enslaved.
They, we could never enslave American Americans.
They, they'd rather die.
And, uh, so they, then they kind of, yeah, didn't lost respect for the blacks because of that.

Um, but, um, and, and the Americans, native Americans were a little angry with the whites for making them cut their hair and, you know, made it so that they couldn't wear their blankets around and, um, that kind of a stuff.
But, um, yeah, so, um, so then chapter seven is early days at Tuskegee.
Tuskegee is a little, um, town, I think in Alabama.
So right there in the deep South.
And, uh, so he, he was enlisted when he was 25 years old, he was enlisted by General Armstrong to start this, uh, a school for black people, uh, in Tuskegee called the Tuskegee University.
It's still a university and, um, they basically had to, you know, build it from scratch.
He and his students, they had to, you know, build up the, the cabin and they had to, um, do the maintenance and do all the farming on it and be totally self-sufficient.
And, um, and, you know, they were really poor, like when it rained, the cabin leaked and his, one of the students would have to hold an umbrella above him.
But, but, uh, so he, his, his job was to be the, uh, the teacher and the, the, the principal of it, president, but also to secure funding.
He needed, um, money for it to go.
So he had to go around and ask, um, all of the people, though, like the people with money, like rich white people for money to keep it going.
And this is where he was, he really liked white people because he was really successful in getting money.
I think he started with like 90,000 or something.
And then by the time he died, he had, he had received collected one and a half million dollars for his school from lots of people.
Um, Armstrong gave him, he said like, you know, his life savings.
And, and, uh, other Christians and church groups would help, uh, money.
And over, over time he, he got, uh, he got, uh, you know, Carnegie and Rockefeller and, uh, Kodak and lots of people to donate.
Um, so chapter eight, teaching school and a stable in Hen House.
Yeah, that was the first place that they, um, that they, uh, first started, uh, teaching.
And, um, but he said that all of the workers were very, all the, the students at the school were very, um, dedicated to the school.
They helped with all the different chores and, um, kept it going.
And, uh, and, um, but he worked really hard.
I think he worked himself to death.
He died at 58, 59 from, like, over work.
Um, and so, let's, let's go to chapter nine.
Anxious days and sleepless nights.
Um, so, so he said that, um, he was just talking about Christmas.
And, uh, I went, he, when he was at the school, he, um, he, he wanted to go on vacation, but he didn't have any money.
So he, he wanted to sell his coat for $3 and he went to a white man and, and, and, and offered it for three bucks.
But the white man said, okay, I'll give you five cents now and then I'll pay you the rest when I can.
And he was like, oh my God.
And I think I've read other books where white people did kind of stuff with black people a lot.
But he said, I can't remember what chapter this was in, but one guy he knew wanted to learn how to play the guitar from a white man.

So the white man said, well, if I'm going to teach you guitar, um, I'll have to charge you, you know, like, um, um, $3 for the first lesson, $2 for the second lesson, and like a dollar for the third lesson and only 40 cents for the last lesson.
And the black guy was like, well, okay, but I'd like to have the last lesson first.
Um, yeah, so let's just run through, um, okay.
Chapter 10, a harder task than making bricks without straw.
So, um, so he's talking about the importance of having students erect their own buildings.
Um, yeah, so he just said that, uh, you know, sometimes he, he cut, he, he saw students, his students, like marking up their desks, you know, like vandalizing the stool.

And he was like, so he, he, he kind of stressed that they would kind of take part in the school and help build the school and maintain the school themselves to make it feel like it was part of theirs.
His philosophy about black people was, he was really smart.
He knew, you know, that you should take baby steps to gain, gain the trust of the white man.
Um, was that, uh, black people needed to kind of, um, prove that they could be independent and, uh, and responsible and be just like a white.
He said that whenever like a white person would start a business venture or school or something that everybody just expected him to be successful.
But if a black person tried, then everybody would expect him to be unsuccessful.
So that's why they tried really extra hard and took it really seriously.
And, um, and, uh, did a pretty good job of, uh, taking care of each other and bringing up the, the school.
So, um, so chapter 10, a harder task.
Oh, wait, that was chapter 10.
Um, so, and another of his philosophies was that, um, that like manual labor was something that was just as respectable as writing a poem.

So he was basically trying to say that, you know, even though blacks were only the workers and, you know, weren't going to try to vote or weren't going to try to, he later, he, he, he wished that blacks would vote.
But, you know, weren't going to try to be the bosses or anything like that.

But even, you know, they were the workers and that you would, you deserve just as much dignity and respect and it had as much value for the world being a worker as, you know, being one of the rich white people.
And, um, and so he tried to instill that value into his students.
And, uh, that's something that we can learn, you know, even today, it kind of seems that we're, as we get in the future, like it becomes,
It's harder and harder for like regular people, you know, like the milkman or somebody to raise a family.
Um, because the wealth discrepancy is getting so much, um, wide, so wide, so much wider.
It's like rich people.

I think the top 1% is like 20% richer than everybody else than it was 20 years ago, 25 years ago in 1978 when they made corporations humans so that they could legally lobby on behalf of the person, the corporation and, um, some other stuff that they did.

Um, but, uh, yeah, back in the day, you know, like in the fifties, like, you know, just the laborer could, could, could, you know, raise a family, would have just as much, you know, respect in the community and stuff.
Now it kind of seems like if you're a laborer, you're, you're just making enough money to survive.
One statistic is, you know, our productivity has gone up a whole bunch since the mid seventies.
Um, and, um, if, if our, uh, minimum wage had kept up with productivity, because productivity makes everybody more money.
It makes, it makes the rich people more money, but they've been hogging more and more money as we go on through time for the last 25 years.
That if we'd have kept minimum wage up to productivity as it was in the past, the minimum wage would be 20 bucks an hour now.
So it's just another illustration of how a wealth conscription just keeps getting larger.

But, you know, in the past, like, you know, you could be like the, you know, the milkman and, and, you know, still be able to go to the same church and have your kids go to the same school and raise your kids.
And you'd be just another worker, you know, the doctor, the doctor would be richer, but it would still be the same.
And, but it kind of seems now that you're, you're just like a loser if you're just a worker.
And anyways, it's just a good system of values, um, to kind of, you know, have, have, uh, real equal respect for everybody.
And that's what he was stressing.
You know, he's saying, you know, don't worry, the black man's not going to try to take your job, but, but you at least have to, uh, have, uh, you know, respect for the black man as a person.
Um, and he really, he really, uh, drew that home when he gave a really famous speech, um, called the, yeah, at the Atlanta Exposition Address.
And that's chapter 14.
Um, so let's see the last one though.
It was chapter 10.
So chapter 11, making their beds before they could lie in them.
Um, so that was just, um, discussing the, the, the growth of his school that he made in Tuskegee and, um, and that, you know, it wasn't his school.
It was our school.
We have to work together.
And so he was, uh, anyway, he, he was very happy about how good of a job all of his workers were doing and his, and his family.
He had three wives, uh, two of them died and, and then his third wife outlived him.
He had kids from his first two wives, but, um, but, uh, but yeah, he was really impressed with, with the white people who would help donate money in the North.
He said a lot of people, uh, donated, a lot of rich people donated money without anybody knowing.
They didn't need anybody to know.
And he said a lot of rich people he knew who donated a lot of money were actually, um, like criticized for not donating money.
Um, so he, and he knew that they did.

And, and he, he just defended them that he said that like, you know, when you, when he, you just, people would go to ask money that they'd get like 20 requests a day through, um, written requests for this, that, and the next thing.
And then he'd be, they'd always all, every day have people waiting in line to try to get money too.

And he said that, you know, don't try to think that they're bad just because they're, um, you know, not giving you money because if they gave all their money away, they couldn't, they couldn't keep their businesses going and stuff.
So, um, that's kind of, uh, one reason all the white, he's liked him a lot.

Um, so, uh, but he got, yeah, a lot of, a lot of, uh, donations from Carnegie and, uh, but Carnegie, you know, wanted to, wanted to know exactly what the money was spent on and, you know, was really, really involved and everything.
Um, yeah, so, uh, and he traveled all over the place.
He was constantly traveling to try to get, um, keep it going, keep the school going.
So that's chapter 12, chapter 13, 2000 miles for a five minute speech.
Um, after his first wife died, he got married again.

And then, um, he, um, he went on a public speaking tour to just, uh, I guess, you know, it sprouted out from his traveling around asking for money, telling about a school to just talking about education for blacks.

And the, and the, and the reason why blacks and whites should work together, they could help each other and that whites shouldn't, you know, try to suppress blacks from, you know, learning how to, to do trades and to, you know, learn liberal arts and this kind of stuff.
That it was good for everybody.

He said that, um, and I've heard this from, you know, Thomas Jefferson from, um, I can't think of that other writer, but I heard it before that, um, that slavery doesn't, it's not just bad for the black people.
It's also bad for the white people.
Um, when the, when the slave has to be held in the ditch, the white man has to be in the ditch there with him, holding him down.

And, um, I can picture that as just, uh, people, um, white people, you know, if you're, if you're don't treat a slave like a human being and you're, and you're cheating, you know, black people out of their money a lot, which happened a lot.
I've read in the other books, you know, like one slave went free and, um, and was going to work to own, buy his land off of the, the, you know, some white guy.
And he paid off the land like five times, but the white guy just kept, kept selling it over and over again.
Cause he knew he'd get away with it.
Cause a black man was illiterate and, um, yeah, so just, uh, so he said that, he said that that would just tempt white people into becoming more and more criminals.
And by the end, they would just be cheating out other white men.
So it just basically turned him into scumbags, the whole slave slavery institution.
And so it helped everybody to, to free them and give, and it would help everybody if they would cooperate and the white people would be fair to the black person.
Um, so, um, so he, yeah, 2000 miles for a moment of speech.
He traveled 2000 miles and gave a five minute speech cause he just didn't have time to keep going.
He was really busy all the time, um, going to the North and all over.
And chapter 14 is the Atlanta exposition address.
This was a, uh, one of the, you know, more like, I think there is a website I read on the Wikipedia that there's a website said all the most important speeches,
Like of the 20th century and his was one of them.
Um, he was the first black person to give a speech to white people, like an actual speech where he wouldn't be interrupted and everybody would listen to him.
This was in, I think, 1901.
No, no, no.
It was in 1895.
He wrote the book in 1901.
So, um, he was active.
He was really influential from 1890 to 1915.
